name: Scarlatos et al. Tutoring Dialogue Simulation Quality Extract
population_or_context: Simulated students in math tutoring dialogues compared with real tutoring dialogue data.
sample_size: The arXiv abstract reports construction of a real-world math tutoring dialogue dataset; the staged pass does not extract exact turn or participant counts.
methods: Extracted the multi-dimensional simulated-student evaluation framework spanning linguistic, behavioral, and cognitive fidelity, with automated and human validation.
education_context: Math tutoring dialogue simulation for intelligent tutoring systems.
response_origin: Prompt-based and post-training simulated students are evaluated against real-world math tutoring dialogue data.
rubric_label_space: Dialogue-level fidelity metrics rather than a single correctness rubric.
label_source: Real tutoring dialogues and human validation provide comparison evidence for automated metric behavior.
generation_model: Prompt-based and post-training LLM simulated-student strategies are evaluated; exact model details require full-text review beyond this staged summary.
generation_method: Prompt-based simulated students and post-training strategies, including SFT and DPO, are compared.
prompt_strategy: Prompt-based simulation is compared with post-training approaches.
evaluation_method: Quality is evaluated with metrics for linguistic, behavioral, and cognitive fidelity, benchmarked against real dialogue data and validated with human judgments.
scorer_use: Tutoring dialogue simulator quality benchmarking; not automated scorer validation.
real_response_comparator: Real-world math tutoring dialogues serve as the comparator for simulated-student fidelity.
reported_metrics: The source reports a comprehensive metric framework and human validation; this staged pass does not extract exact metric values.
limitations: The source is dialogue-simulation evidence rather than standalone assessment-response scoring evidence.
The staged pass captures the metric framework and top-line comparison, not every benchmark value.
The paper reports that prompt-based simulated students often fail to simulate diverse realistic students and post-training narrows but does not close the gap.
